items.title: ninejs: Bringing interactivity to plotnine
items.text: ninejs adds interactive behavior to plotnine charts with a minimal, composable API. You can attach tooltips, hover grouping, and other frontend interactions directly from aes(...), then export the result as a standalone HTML plot.

items.title: Quick start
items.text:
Specify the tooltip and data_id aesthetic mappings, and then pass your plotnine chart to interactive():

from plotnine import ggplot, aes, geom_point, theme_minimal
from plotnine.data import anscombe_quartet

from ninejs import interactive, css, save

gg = (
    ggplot(
        data=anscombe_quartet,
        mapping=aes(x="x", y="y", color="dataset", tooltip="dataset", data_id="dataset"),
    )
    + geom_point(size=7, alpha=0.5)
    + theme_minimal()
)

(
    interactive(gg)
    + css(from_dict={".tooltip": {"font-size": "3em"}})
    + save("docs/iframes/quickstart2.html")
)

items.title: Installation
items.text:
pipuvpixi 
pip install ninejs

uv add ninejs

pixi add ninejs

items.title: Examples
items.text:
TooltipGroupingLine chartBarplot 
from plotnine import ggplot, aes, geom_point, theme_minimal
from plotnine.data import anscombe_quartet

from ninejs import interactive, css, save

gg = (
    ggplot(data=anscombe_quartet, mapping=aes(x="x", y="y", tooltip="dataset"))
    + geom_point(size=7, alpha=0.5)
    + theme_minimal()
)

(
    interactive(gg)
    + css(".tooltip {font-size: 2em;}")
    + save("docs/iframes/point.html")
)

from plotnine import ggplot, aes, geom_point, theme_minimal
from plotnine.data import anscombe_quartet

from ninejs import interactive, css, save

gg = (
    ggplot(
        data=anscombe_quartet,
        mapping=aes(x="x", y="y", color="dataset", tooltip="dataset", data_id="dataset"),
    )
    + geom_point(size=7, alpha=0.5)
    + theme_minimal()
)

(
    interactive(gg)
    + css(from_dict={".tooltip": {"font-size": "3em"}})
    + save("docs/iframes/quickstart2.html")
)

gg = (
    ggplot(
        data=anscombe_quartet,
        mapping=aes(x="x", y="y", color="dataset", tooltip="dataset"),
    )
    + geom_line(size=4, alpha=0.5)
    + theme_minimal()
)

(
    interactive(gg)
    + css(from_dict={".tooltip": {"font-size": "3em"}})
    + save("docs/iframes/line.html")
)

df = pd.DataFrame({"category": ["A", "B", "C"], "value": [3, 7, 5]})
df["tooltip"] = df["category"].astype(str) + " (" + df["value"].astype(str) + ")"


gg = (
    ggplot(df, aes(x="category", y="value", tooltip="tooltip"))
    + geom_col()
    + theme_classic()
)

interactive(gg) + save("docs/iframes/bar.html")

items.title: css
items.text:
Utility class to handle CSS injection for interactive plots.

This class provides multiple ways to load CSS: directly from a string, from a dictionary, or from a CSS file. It is intended to be combined with interactive plots.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default from_string str

CSS rules in a string.

None from_dict str

Dictionnary containing selectors as keys, and dictionnaries of property-value pairs as values.

None from_file str

Path to a CSS file.

None 
(
    interactive(p)
    + css(".tooltip: {font-size: 2rem}")
    + css(css_from_dict={".tooltip": {"font-size": "2rem"})
    + css(from_file="style.css")
    + save("output.html")
)

items.title: is_css_like(s)
items.text:
Check whether a string looks like valid CSS. This function is primarly used internally, but you can use it too.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default s str

A string to evaluate.

required

Returns:

Type Description bool

Whether or not s looks like valid CSS.

Examples:

from plotjs import is_css_like

is_css_like("This is not CSS.") # False
is_css_like(".box { broken }") # False
is_css_like(".tooltip { color: red; background: blue; }") # True

items.title: Interactive
items.text:
Wrapper for a plotnine ggplot object to make it interactive.

It automatically extracts tooltips and grouping information from the plot mapping if present.

Attributes:

Name Type Description gg ggplot

The original plotnine ggplot object.

Example 
from plotnine import ggplot, aes, geom_point
from ninejs import interactive, css, save

p = ggplot(df, aes("x", "y", tooltip="label")) + geom_point()
(
    interactive(p)
    + css(from_file="style.css")
    + save("chart.html")
)

items.title: javascript
items.text:
Utility class to handle JavaScript injection for interactive plots.

This class provides multiple ways to load JavasScript: directly from a string or from a JavaScript file. It is intended to be combined with interactive plots.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default from_string str

JavaScript in a string.

None from_file str

Path to a JavaScript file.

None 
(
    interactive(p)
    + javascript("console.log('hello world')")
    + javascript(from_file="script.js")
    + save("output.html")
)

items.title: Save
items.text:
Utility class to specify an output HTML file for saving an interactive plot.

Example: 

(
    interactive(p)
    + css(from_file="style.css")
    + save("output.html")
)
